questions.question: Why should you try vertical scaling before horizontal scaling?
questions.expectedAnswer: Vertical scaling (bigger machine) is operationally simpler -- no distributed systems complexity, no data partitioning, no network partitions to handle. It has a ceiling, but reaching that ceiling means you have a good problem. Only go horizontal when vertical is exhausted or insufficient.
questions.rubric: Must explain operational simplicity advantage and the complexity cost of horizontal

questions.question: What makes data the hardest thing to scale?
questions.expectedAnswer: Compute can be scaled by adding stateless instances. But data requires consistency, replication, partitioning, and conflict resolution -- all of which introduce complexity. CAP theorem means you cannot have perfect consistency, availability, and partition tolerance simultaneously.
questions.rubric: Must address consistency challenges and why data differs from compute
